title: Roast beef sirloin
subtitle: Salt-rubbed sirloin rested and carved pink
excerpt: A whole sirloin roasted at high heat for a crust, then at low heat to the right internal temperature, rested and carved pink. The technique is straightforward: accurate temperature, generous seasoning, and patience with the rest. Served with Yorkshire puddings, roast potatoes, and red-wine gravy.
sourceNotes: The Sunday sirloin is the centrepiece of the British roast tradition. The cut, the temperature, and the resting are the three things that determine success. Mrs Beeton and Escoffier both insist on proper resting; modern writers follow suit. A meat thermometer is the reliable check here — colour and time are unreliable guides.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Take the beef from the fridge 1 hour before roasting.
recipe.temperatureNote: 200°C fan / Gas 7 for initial blast, then 160°C / 140°C fan

content.text: A sirloin joint of around 1.5 kg feeds six people generously. Buy bone-in if possible — the bone conducts heat and adds flavour. Season generously with salt the day before and leave uncovered in the fridge; the salt draws out moisture and then the surface dries overnight, giving a better crust.

Internal temperatures for beef: 50°C for rare, 55°C for medium-rare, 60°C for medium. Pull the joint from the oven 5°C below your target — it rises during resting. Rest for at least 20 minutes under loose foil. Carving too soon loses all the juices.

items.prepNote: rolled or bone-in, at room temperature
applied the night before if possible

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C. Rub the {{beef-sirloin}} all over with {{vegetable-oil}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Place fat-side up in a roasting tin. Roast at 220°C for 20 minutes to form a crust. Reduce the oven to 160°C and continue roasting for about 60 minutes for medium-rare in a 1.5 kg joint. Use a meat thermometer: 50°C for rare, 55°C for medium-rare, 60°C for medium. Remove from the oven 5°C below your target. Transfer to a warm board, cover loosely with foil, and rest for 20 minutes. Carve across the grain into slices. Use the resting juices and roasting tin drippings for gravy.

items.symptom: Grey ring around the outside with a cold centre
items.cause: Oven too hot or beef too cold when it went in
items.fix: Always start with room-temperature beef. The grey ring is overcooked outer meat — reduce the high-blast time and lower the temperature sooner
20 minutes minimum under foil. If carving produces a flood of juice, the joint needed longer — cover and wait another 10 minutes

items.cause: Surface wet, or oven not hot enough for the blast phase
items.fix: Pat the beef completely dry before oiling. The 220°C blast must be done in a fully preheated oven for the Maillard crust to form

content.text: Roast beef is the centrepiece of the British Sunday lunch tradition. Sirloin is the standard cut for a smaller gathering; a rib on the bone is the grander version for a crowd. The ritual of roast beef with Yorkshire pudding, roast potatoes, and gravy is one of the most deeply embedded food traditions in British life. It marks Sunday, celebrations, and family gatherings with an unmistakable combination of smells and expectation.
